questions.stem: A set $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is open if:
options: it contains all of its limit points.
for each $x \in U$ there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ with $(x - \varepsilon, x + \varepsilon) \subseteq U$.
questions.explanation: Openness means every point of $U$ is interior: each $x \in U$ has some $\varepsilon$-neighborhood entirely inside $U$. Containing all limit points is the definition of closed, not open.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which of these subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is closed?
questions.explanation: $[0,1]$ contains all of its limit points (including the endpoints $0$ and $1$), so it is closed; equivalently its complement $(-\infty,0)\cup(1,\infty)$ is open. The others each omit a limit point, so they are not closed.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: A point $x$ is an accumulation (limit) point of a set $S$ if:
options: every neighborhood of $x$ contains a point of $S$ different from $x$.
questions.explanation: An accumulation point $x$ has the property that every neighborhood meets $S$ in a point other than $x$, so points of $S$ cluster arbitrarily close to $x$. Note $x$ need not belong to $S$, and isolated points are exactly the non-accumulation points of $S$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The Heine-Borel theorem states that a subset $K$ of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact if and only if:
questions.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}$ (and $\mathbb{R}^n$), Heine-Borel characterizes compactness as closed and bounded. Either condition alone is insufficient: $[0,\infty)$ is closed but unbounded, and $(0,1)$ is bounded but not closed, and neither is compact.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem states that:
options: every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
questions.explanation: Bolzano-Weierstrass: any bounded sequence of reals has a convergent subsequence. Equivalently, every bounded infinite subset of $\mathbb{R}$ has at least one accumulation point. Boundedness is essential.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: What is the interior of the set $[0, 1]$?
questions.explanation: The interior is the largest open set contained in $[0,1]$, namely $(0,1)$. The endpoints $0$ and $1$ are not interior because every neighborhood of them sticks out of $[0,1]$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: What is the closure of the open interval $(0, 1)$?
questions.explanation: The closure adds all limit points. For $(0,1)$ the limit points include the endpoints, so the closure is $[0,1]$, the smallest closed set containing $(0,1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The only accumulation point of the set $S = \left\{ \dfrac{1}{n} : n \in \mathbb{N} \right\}$ is:
questions.explanation: The terms $1/n$ pile up only near $0$, so $0$ is the single accumulation point (and $0 \notin S$). Each $1/n$ is isolated, with a small neighborhood containing no other point of $S$, so the points of $S$ are not accumulation points.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact?
questions.explanation: By Heine-Borel a set is compact iff closed and bounded. $\{1/n\}\cup\{0\}$ is bounded and closed (it contains its only limit point $0$), hence compact. $(0,1)$ is not closed, $[0,\infty)$ is unbounded, and $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is not closed in $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The closure of $\mathbb{Q}$ (the rationals) in $\mathbb{R}$ is:
questions.explanation: Because $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense, every real number is a limit of rationals, so the closure of $\mathbb{Q}$ is all of $\mathbb{R}$. (Correspondingly, the interior of $\mathbb{Q}$ is empty.)
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which two subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ are simultaneously open and closed?
questions.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}$ the only clopen sets are $\emptyset$ and $\mathbb{R}$ itself; this is exactly the statement that $\mathbb{R}$ is connected. A singleton is closed but not open, and $\mathbb{Q}$ is neither.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: How many accumulation points does a finite set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ have?
options: the same as the number of elements.
questions.explanation: A finite set has no accumulation points: around each point you can take a neighborhood small enough to exclude the finitely many other points. So every point of a finite set is isolated, and there are no limit points.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The set $\mathbb{Z}$ of integers, as a subset of $\mathbb{R}$, is:
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Z}$ is closed (its complement is a union of open intervals) and has no accumulation points. But it is unbounded, so by Heine-Borel it is not compact. It is not open since no neighborhood of an integer lies inside $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: A point $x \in S$ is an isolated point of $S$ if:
options: there is a neighborhood of $x$ containing no point of $S$ other than $x$.
every neighborhood of $x$ meets $S$ in infinitely many points.
questions.explanation: An isolated point has a neighborhood whose only intersection with $S$ is $x$ itself. This is the exact opposite of being an accumulation point, where every neighborhood meets $S$ in some other point.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Why is the open interval $(0, 1)$ NOT compact?
options: The open cover $\left\{ \left( \tfrac{1}{n}, 1 \right) : n \in \mathbb{N} \right\}$ has no finite subcover.
questions.explanation: Compactness requires every open cover to have a finite subcover. The cover by $(\tfrac1n, 1)$ covers $(0,1)$, but any finite subfamily only reaches down to some $\tfrac1N > 0$, missing points near $0$. So no finite subcover exists, and $(0,1)$ is not compact.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The interior of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$ is:
questions.explanation: No interval lies entirely inside $\mathbb{Q}$ because every interval contains irrationals. Hence $\mathbb{Q}$ has no interior points and its interior is empty.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The closure of a set $S$ can be described as:
options: the largest open set contained in $S$.
$S$ together with all of its accumulation points.
the set of isolated points of $S$.
questions.explanation: The closure $\overline{S}$ is $S \cup S'$, where $S'$ is the set of accumulation points; equivalently it is the smallest closed set containing $S$. (The largest open set inside $S$ is the interior, a different object.)
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which statement about unions and intersections of open sets is TRUE in $\mathbb{R}$?
options: An arbitrary union of closed sets is closed.
An arbitrary union of open sets is open.
An arbitrary intersection of open sets is always open.
A finite union of closed sets is always open.
questions.explanation: Any union of open sets is open. Finite intersections of open sets are open, but arbitrary intersections need not be (for example $\bigcap_n (-\tfrac1n, \tfrac1n) = \{0\}$ is closed). Dually, arbitrary intersections of closed sets are closed but arbitrary unions need not be.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: By Bolzano-Weierstrass, the bounded sequence $x_n = (-1)^n + \dfrac{1}{n}$ has convergent subsequences with limits:
questions.explanation: The sequence is bounded, so Bolzano-Weierstrass guarantees convergent subsequences. The even terms $1 + \tfrac1n \to 1$ and the odd terms $-1 + \tfrac1n \to -1$, so the subsequential limits are $1$ and $-1$. (Divergence of the full sequence does not prevent convergent subsequences.)
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: An accumulation point of a set $S$:
options: must be an interior point of $S$.
questions.explanation: Accumulation points need not lie in the set: $0$ is an accumulation point of $\{1/n\}$ but is not a member. (Closed sets are exactly those that do contain all their accumulation points.)
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The set $S = (1, 2) \cup \{3\}$ has interior equal to:
questions.explanation: The interior keeps only points with a full neighborhood inside $S$. Every point of $(1,2)$ qualifies, but the isolated point $3$ has no neighborhood contained in $S$, so it is excluded. The interior is $(1,2)$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: A continuous real-valued function on a compact set $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is guaranteed to:
options: attain a maximum and a minimum on $K$.
questions.explanation: The Extreme Value Theorem: a continuous function on a compact set attains its supremum and infimum. Compactness (closed and bounded, by Heine-Borel) is what guarantees the max and min are achieved.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: The closure of the set $S = \left\{ \dfrac{1}{n} : n \in \mathbb{N} \right\}$ is:
questions.explanation: The only accumulation point is $0$, which is not already in $S$. Adding it gives $\overline{S} = S \cup \{0\}$. This is closed, since it now contains its single limit point.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which condition on a bounded infinite set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is guaranteed by Bolzano-Weierstrass?
options: $S$ has at least one accumulation point.
questions.explanation: The set form of Bolzano-Weierstrass: every bounded infinite subset of $\mathbb{R}$ has an accumulation point. It need not be closed, compact, or an interval (for example $\{1/n\}$ is bounded and infinite with accumulation point $0$, yet is none of those).
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Classify the interval $S = [2, 5)$ as a subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.solution: The set $S$ is not open, because the point $2$ has no neighborhood lying entirely inside $S$. It is also not closed, because the limit point $5$ is missing. Its interior is the open interval with left endpoint {{1}} and right endpoint {{2}}. The single limit point that must be added to form the closure is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The interior of $[2,5)$ is $(2,5)$, so the endpoints are $2$ and $5$. The closure is $[2,5]$, which requires adjoining the missing limit point $5$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Consider the set $S = \left\{ 2 + \dfrac{1}{n} : n \in \mathbb{N} \right\}$.
questions.solution: The terms decrease toward $2$, so the unique accumulation point of $S$ is the number {{1}}. Since this point does not belong to $S$, the set $S$ is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The values $2 + \tfrac1n$ cluster only at $2$, the single accumulation point. Because $2 \notin S$, the set fails to contain its limit point, so it is not closed.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Decide whether the set $K = [0, 1] \cup [2, 3]$ is compact, using the Heine-Borel theorem.
questions.solution: The set $K$ is bounded, since it lies inside the interval $[0, 3]$, and it is closed, being a finite union of closed intervals. The number of separate closed intervals making up $K$ is {{1}}. By Heine-Borel, a closed and bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact, so $K$ {{2}} compact.
questions.explanation: $K$ is the union of $2$ closed intervals, hence closed, and is contained in $[0,3]$, hence bounded. Heine-Borel then gives that $K$ is compact.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis

questions.stem: Analyze the unbounded set $S = [4, \infty)$ for compactness.
questions.solution: The set $S$ is closed, since its complement $(-\infty, 4)$ is open. However $S$ is {{1}}, because it extends without bound to the right. By the Heine-Borel theorem a compact set must be both closed and bounded, so $S$ {{2}} compact.
questions.explanation: $[4,\infty)$ is closed but unbounded. Heine-Borel requires both closed and bounded for compactness, so the failure of boundedness means $S$ is not compact.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle and Sherbert, Introduction to Real Analysis
